front: Work: argues a ruler must be feared rather than loved, tame Fortuna (compared to a raging river and a woman), and avoid mercenaries; holds up Cesare Borgia as a model
Work: identifies Theseus, Moses, Romulus, and Cyrus as 'outstanding men' who attained power through virtu; contrasts armed vs. unarmed prophets
Work: Frederick the Great published a critique of this treatise, edited by Voltaire, shortly after being elected king
Work: a commentary on Livy's Ab Urbe Condita espousing Roman republicanism; contrasts Athens with Sparta and argues republics are superior because Rome could switch between Fabius and Scipio
Philosopher: Leo Strauss called this thinker the 'first of the moderns' and analyzed his 'anti-theological ire' as the source of modernity in Thoughts on this thinker
Philosopher: Harvey Mansfield argued this thinker sought to overthrow Christianity; James Burnham's book on this thinker traces his influence through Robert Michels
Work: Callimaco tricks the infertile Nicia by disguising himself as a doctor to administer a fake fertility potion; the marriage-broker Ligurio helps the scheme
Philosopher: the ghost of 'Machiavel' opens Marlowe's The Jew of Malta declaring 'religion but a childish toy'; in Henry VI Part 3, Richard III can set this 'murderous' person 'to school'
Philosopher: imprisoned after the Sack of Prato; complained of farm life in letters to Francesco Vettori; J.G.A. Pocock links him with Guicciardini in The Machiavellian Moment
Philosopher: adapted Terence's Andria for the Florentine stage and wrote Clizia (based on a lost Plautus play) and the novella 'Belfagor the Archdemon'
